Research Companion · Publication-Ready Study Design

From Brewing Hypothesis to Published Science

A plain-language guide to the Kinetic Bypass Over-Pour (KBO-P) research package — how to take a coffee brewing intuition all the way to a Q1 peer-reviewed journal.

01What is KBO-P, really?

A brewing protocol — and a falsifiable scientific claim

Kinetic Bypass Over-Pour (KBO-P) is a four-phase pour-over brewing protocol. The brewing itself is straightforward — anyone with a V60, a scale, and a sieve can execute it. What makes it research-worthy is the underlying claim: that by separating the fine grind particles from the bulk and pouring water of a specific cooled temperature onto them at a specific moment, you can selectively extract the desirable compounds (acids, sugars) while leaving the bitter compounds (CGA lactones, polyphenols) behind.

"Not just a recipe — a falsifiable hypothesis about which molecules come out of a coffee bed under which thermal conditions."

The intuition is old in specialty coffee. The novelty here is treating it like a chemistry problem and giving it a quantitative spine: Arrhenius kinetics, Fick's diffusion law, and a deterministic temperature program created by passive cooling in a polymer vessel. Once it has a quantitative spine, it can be tested. Once it can be tested, it can fail. That is what makes it science.

Why a polymer vessel?

A standard kettle holds its temperature. A Tritan™ plastic vessel, by contrast, has low thermal conductivity and high heat capacity — water poured into it at 94 °C cools predictably to around 76 °C in 90 seconds. That cooling curve is your second kettle, for free. No active temperature control, no dilution, no extra equipment.

02Why does this need a study?

Because intuition is cheap; data is what reviewers read.

Coffee competitions are full of confident claims. Most of them have never been tested against a control. "I brew this way and it tastes better" is a personal report. "I brew this way and 8 blinded judges score it 1.5 SD higher than the standard protocol across 48 randomized brews, with HPLC confirming a 30% drop in 5-CQA lactone" — that is a publishable scientific finding.

The gap between those two statements is the experimental design. This package contains everything needed to bridge it. Without it, KBO-P is craft wisdom. With it, KBO-P is a piece of food science that can be cited, replicated, built on, or refuted by others.

What "published in Q1" actually means

Journals like the Journal of Agricultural and Food Chemistry (impact factor 4.8) or Food Chemistry (IF 8.8) accept fewer than 25% of submissions. To survive peer review there, three things matter most:

  • Pre-registration — the hypothesis must be locked before data collection, to prevent unconscious cherry-picking.
  • Falsifiability — the paper must clearly state what outcome would prove the hypothesis wrong.
  • Mechanism + measurement — both the chemistry (why) and the analytical readout (HPLC, sensory) must be present.

The package is structured so that every one of these boxes is ticked before the first brew happens.

03The mechanism in three ideas

Three engineering primitives, one selectivity gain.

01
Spatial segregation

Sieve the grind at 300 μm. The fines fraction (~13% of the dose) has a huge surface area per gram — perfect for fast, low-temperature extraction. Hold them in reserve.

02
Temporal segregation

Use a high-turbulence spiral pour at 30–60 s to thin the boundary layer and release the easy desirables. Then go quiet from 60–90 s to let diffusion do the work without disturbing the bed.

03
Thermal segregation

At 90 s, drop the reserved fines onto the bed and pour 76 °C water over them — water that has been passively cooling in a plastic vessel since the brew began. Tail-cut at 1:50.

Each primitive on its own does something useful. The novelty of the protocol — and the reason it needs four treatments to test, not just two — is the claim that the three combined produce more than the sum of their parts.

The math behind the temperature drop

The reason 76 °C water selectively suppresses undesirables comes from the Arrhenius equation. Compounds with higher activation energies are more sensitive to temperature changes. Going from 94 °C to 76 °C slows polyphenol release by roughly 1.87× more than it slows citric acid release.

Selectivity ratio = exp[(ΔEₐ / R) × (1/T_cold − 1/T_hot)]
Arrhenius spread between high-Eₐ and low-Eₐ compounds

Combined with the very short diffusion path inside fines particles (where low-Eₐ compounds reach equilibrium in 3–4 seconds), this gives a window where desirables come out and undesirables don't. That window is what KBO-P is engineered to exploit.

Honest framing

The suppression is kinetic, not absolute. The protocol slows undesirable extraction by ~1.87× — it does not "halt" it. Anyone claiming "complete bypass" of bitter compounds is overselling. We frame it as a selectivity gain over a finite time window. That framing is what survives peer review.

04Four falsifiable hypotheses

Locked before the first brew. No moving goalposts.

A pre-registered hypothesis is one whose outcome criterion is published before the data exist. This is what separates a confirmation bias detector from a fishing expedition.

H1 · Non-inferiority of desirables
KBO-P will preserve at least 95% of the conventional protocol's free sugar yield.
T1 sugars ≥ 0.95 × C1 sugars, p < 0.0125
H2 · Bitter compound suppression
KBO-P will reduce 5-CQA lactone (the "Starbucks bitter" precursor) by at least 30%.
T1 lactone ≤ 0.70 × C1, p < 0.0125
H3 · Sensory differentiation
Blinded judges will perceive KBO-P as sweeter-and-less-bitter than the control.
Sweet:bitter ratio T1 − C1 ≥ 1.5 SD, p < 0.0125
H4 · Flavor stability over time
KBO-P will hold its quality better across the 15-minute cooling curve.
FSI(T1) ≥ 1.20 × FSI(C1), p < 0.0125
Decision rule

Strong evidence means H1 + H2 + (H3 or H4) all pass. Partial confirmation means H1 + H2 pass but the sensory translation doesn't show up. Null result means H1 or H2 fails. All three outcomes are publishable, just in different journals with different framings. None of them is a failure of the package — only a finding.

05The experimental design

Four treatments, not two. Here is why.

If the only comparison were KBO-P vs conventional, and KBO-P won, you would not know which part of KBO-P did the work. Was it the fines segregation? The two-temperature delivery? The combination? Without that, the paper has no mechanism. The four-treatment design decomposes the effect.

C1 · Conventional
Uniform 94 °C, unsifted, 3 pours

The familiar 3-pour V60. This is the baseline everyone in specialty coffee already knows. If KBO-P doesn't beat this, the protocol has no commercial reason to exist.

C2 · Sifted control
Uniform 94 °C, fines removed, otherwise conventional

Tests whether simply removing fines (the standard "channeling fix") captures the benefit. If C2 ≈ T1, then the temperature step is irrelevant.

C3 · Two-temperature control
94 °C for early phases, 76 °C terminal pour, unsifted

Tests whether two-temperature delivery alone (without fines segregation) captures the benefit. If C3 ≈ T1, then the sieve is irrelevant.

T1 · Full KBO-P
All three primitives combined

The novel protocol. The four-way comparison shows whether the combination is genuinely synergistic — or whether one of the controls already captures most of the benefit.

Numbers in brief

  • 48 brewing sessions total (4 treatments × 6 replicates × 2 operators)
  • 24 brewing days, 2 brews per day, order randomized
  • 8 blinded sensory judges, each scoring every brew at 6 time points
  • 2304 individual sensory data cells when complete (48 × 8 × 6)
  • 7 compound classes quantified by HPLC for each brew
  • α = 0.0125 per test (Bonferroni-adjusted across 4 primary contrasts)
06What the model predicts

If the kinetic theory is right, this is what we should see.

These figures are model predictions — what should appear if the hypothesis is correct. After data collection, we will replace them with real measurements. Any large discrepancy between prediction and observation becomes itself a finding worth discussing.

Figure 1
Extraction trajectories under each protocol. The control runs to 4 minutes; KBO-P tail-cuts at 1:50. The undesirables (dashed lines: 5-CQA lactone, polyphenols) are still climbing at the cut — they never reach their plateau. The desirables (solid lines: citric acid, sugars) are already at 80–90% of their plateau by 90 seconds. Stopping early sacrifices a little desirable yield to avoid a lot of undesirable accumulation.
Figure 2
The selectivity outcome at a glance. KBO-P loses 8–10% on the desirable side (acceptable) but suppresses 5-CQA lactone by ~21%. That asymmetric trade is exactly what hypothesis H2 predicts. The percentages here are the model's prediction; the study will measure the real numbers.
Figure 3
How the plastic vessel becomes your "second kettle." Newton's law of cooling with time constant τ ≈ 480 s predicts the centerline temperature at the moment of the Phase C pour. The shaded band shows the operator-to-operator variance. The blue dot marks 90 seconds in — exactly where Phase C begins, exactly where we want 76 °C water.
Figure 4
Predicted Flavor Stability Index across the cooling curve. Single-timepoint sensory scoring (just "what does it taste like now?") misses the most important quality dimension — what happens to the cup over 15 minutes. FSI is the area-under-curve, normalized to 100. The protocol's selling point: KBO-P should not just taste better at t = 0, it should hold its quality longer.
Figure 5
The Arrhenius spread tells you how aggressive your temperature step can be. The map shows the relative kinetic suppression between high-Eₐ and low-Eₐ compounds as a function of how big a temperature drop you take and how large the Eₐ difference is. KBO-P operates at a deliberately modest point (1.87×) — large enough to matter, small enough to be reproducible.
Figure 6
Why 300 μm is the right sieve cut. The diffusion time inside a particle scales with R². Below 300 μm, particles equilibrate in seconds even at 76 °C — short enough to fit inside the 10-second Phase C window. Above 300 μm, equilibrium takes minutes. The sieve is the dividing line between "fast enough" and "too slow."
07Eight-week execution timeline

From pre-registration to submission, in two months.

Week 1 · Setup
Equipment calibration
Refractometer against 8% sucrose standard. Grinder verified against Kruve sieves to confirm D₅₀ = 750 μm. Thermocouples checked at ice point and boiling point. Water chemistry confirmed by titration.
Week 2 · Calibration
Sensory panel + pre-registration
Recruit and calibrate the 8-judge panel against a commercial standardized reference cold brew. Lock all four hypotheses on OSF.io. Do not collect any data until the pre-registration is timestamped.
Weeks 3–6 · Execution
48 randomized brews
Two brews per day, treatment order randomized per the locked schedule. Each brew: brewing log → refractometer → pH → freeze 5 mL aliquot. Twice a week, the sensory panel cups eight brews each. Daily QC checks logged.
Week 7 · Analytical
HPLC batch + statistical analysis
All 48 frozen aliquots run in randomized order. Compound concentrations entered into the workbook; Master_Dataset auto-populates. R mixed-effects model fitted per the locked SAP. Hypothesis decisions tabled.
Week 8 · Submission
Manuscript revision + JAFC submission
Predicted figures replaced with measured data. Cover letter finalized. Submission checklist verified. Submit via ACS Paragon Plus with 4 suggested reviewers. Expect first decision in 8–14 weeks.
08Reviewer challenges, pre-empted

Every reasonable critique, anticipated and answered.

A first-time submitter to a Q1 journal usually gets desk-rejected because the obvious objections were not addressed. The package is designed to make each of these objections un-makeable.

Likely critique How the package handles it
"Just craft tuning, not science." Pre-registered hypotheses, explicit falsification criteria, four-treatment decomposition.
"The 'self-brake' claim is overstated." Manuscript explicitly reframes as kinetic, not thermodynamic suppression. Selectivity ratio (1.87×) is calculated, not asserted.
"Is it the fines or the temperature?" C2 vs C3 vs T1 contrasts decompose the effect explicitly. This is the most publishable mechanistic finding regardless of the headline outcome.
"Sensory scoring is noisy." Eight judges × six timepoints × 48 brews. Calibration sample at each session. Mixed-effects model accounts for judge variance.
"You invoke molecular associations without evidence." Removed. The original draft's hydrogen-bonding claim was deleted because it has no direct support. FSI improvement is attributed only to reduced absolute load of lactones and polyphenols.
"Single bean lot limits generalizability." Acknowledged in the Limitations section. A multi-origin extension study is pre-planned as a companion publication.
09What is in the package

Nine files. Everything you need, nothing you don't.

KBO-P_Publication_Package/ ├── 00_README.md overview, execution order, risk mitigation ├── 01_Experimental_Design_Protocol.md treatments, randomization, QC, exclusions ├── 02_Master_Data_Workbook.xlsx 9 tabs, 1,873 formulas, auto-aggregating ├── 03_Statistical_Analysis_Plan.md mixed models, R code, contrast specifications ├── 04_kbo_p_simulation.py kinetic model, figure generation ├── 05_Cover_Letter.md JAFC editor-in-chief template ├── 06_Pre_Registration.md OSF template — lock before first brew ├── 07_Submission_Checklist.md 50+ point ACS submission checklist ├── 08_Sensory_Scoresheet.xlsx print-ready panel form + calibration ref └── figures/ 6 publication figures at 300 dpi

What to do next, in order

  1. Read 00_README.md with all co-authors. Confirm everyone understands the four treatments.
  2. Lock the Statistical Analysis Plan with your statistician. This is the document that cannot change after data collection begins.
  3. Deposit the Pre-Registration on OSF.io. Save the timestamp screenshot. No brewing before this step.
  4. Run Week 1 + Week 2 calibration. Discard pilot brews; they exist to debug protocol mechanics, not to generate data.
  5. Execute the 48-brew schedule. Resist the temptation to look at interim results.
  6. Run HPLC batch and analysis. The hypothesis decisions appear automatically in the workbook's Summary_Stats tab.
  7. Update the manuscript figures with real data. Submit.
The hardest part

The hardest step is not the brewing. It is locking the hypothesis before the data exist and not moving the goalposts when results come in. The package's structure makes goalpost-moving impossible — every decision rule is in a separate document with a timestamp. That is the credibility infrastructure that converts a brewing intuition into a published paper.

This research companion accompanies the Inside the Dripper visualization. Together they form a complete bridge — from understanding what happens inside a coffee bed, to publishing a peer-reviewed claim about it.
